Adversarially review a generated HIGH-LEVEL DESIGN (HLD) interview session. Be a skeptic — find REAL defects, do not rubber-stamp. Review-only: DO NOT modify files.

Workspace: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/084c563c-9047-4d34-a2b8-18502316af3c_hld/_response.md  (+ maybe capacity.py)
Problem: Design a Distributed Cache (Redis-style). Emphasis was: consistent hashing for partitioning + rebalancing on node add/remove, replication (primary-replica) + consistency model, eviction policies, hot-key mitigation (replicate hot shard / client-side cache), cache stampede/thundering herd. Deep dives: consistent hashing + rebalancing, hot key, replication & failover, cache stampede.

Report PASS/FAIL with evidence (section / line) on each:
1. ALL 11 sections present and FULLY EXPANDED (not stubs): §1 Requirements, §2 Clarifying Qs, §3 Scale & Capacity, §4 Entities, §5 API, §6 High-Level Design, §7 Data Model & Storage, §8 Deep Dives Bad→Good→Great, §9 Reliability/Failure/Cost, §10 Trade-off Ledger, §11 Interviewer Q&A. md length > 20,000 chars.
2. CAPACITY MATH (§3): are the numbers actually derived (visible derivation chains) and self-consistent (the arithmetic checks out)? Is there a named decision-forcing number + flip threshold? Any false precision or hand-waving? Re-run capacity.py with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python if present and confirm numbers match.
3. TECHNICAL CORRECTNESS: is the architecture sound for THIS problem and the emphasized focus? Are the deep dives actually the hardest problems (the binding bottleneck, hot-key/celebrity, the global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, idempotency on mutating endpoints)? Find any claim that is wrong or hand-wavy (e.g. "just add a cache" without the miss-QPS reasoning, oversell not actually prevented, consistency hand-waved).
4. MERMAID SAFETY (CRITICAL — HLD has many diagrams): scan EVERY ```mermaid block. FAIL any that contains a label/edge-text with breaking characters: parentheses ( ), square brackets used wrongly, a colon ':' inside an edge/label, a semicolon ';' inside a label, slashes, quotes, angle brackets, or curly braces in node/edge text. These break the renderer. Report the exact offending line per diagram. Also confirm diagrams are flowchart TD (not LR) for multi-path architecture.
5. API (§5): real REST endpoints with JSON examples, idempotency on mutating endpoints, security (caller identity from token)?
6. Data model (§7): stores chosen with the access-pattern justification, partition/shard keys, per-operation consistency?
7. Markdown hygiene: no LaTeX, no stray tool/XML tags, tables well-formed, the prescribed emoji-script structure present.

Return verdict SHIP or FIX-FIRST and a findings list (severity/where/problem/fix). Mermaid breaking-char issues are BLOCKER (they break rendering). Only BLOCKER/MAJOR block; list MINORs too.

content.content:
1	# Design a Distributed Cache (Redis-style)
2	
3	> 🎙️ **Opener:** "We're building a distributed in-memory key-value cache — a Redis-style tier that sits in front of a slower datastore and serves `get`/`put`/`expire` at single-digit-millisecond latency. The interesting part isn't the hash map; it's making that hash map survive being spread across hundreds of machines: partitioning so any node can find any key, replication so a node dying doesn't lose data or drop traffic, eviction so memory never overflows, and surviving hot keys and cache stampedes without melting either the cache or the origin behind it."
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)
8	
9	> 🎙️ **Structure pitch:** "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and the core entities, draw the high-level design one operation at a time, then go deep on the four parts that actually decide whether this works at scale — consistent hashing and rebalancing, hot keys, replication and failover, and cache stampede. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"
10	
11	The core tension: a single in-memory hash map is trivially fast but caps out on one machine's RAM and CPU. The whole job is to **spread that map across a fleet without losing the speed** — and to keep it correct and available while nodes are constantly being added, dying, and getting hammered by skewed traffic.
12	
13	### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")
14	
15	**FR1 — GET a value by key.** A client should be able to ask for a key and get back the value (or a miss) in single-digit milliseconds.
16	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You hand me a label, I hand you back what's stored under it — instantly — or tell you we don't have it."
17	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the key could live on any of hundreds of nodes; the client (or a router) has to know *which* node owns it without asking a central directory on every read.
18	
19	**FR2 — PUT (set) a key-value with optional TTL.** A client should be able to write a value under a key, optionally with a time-to-live after which it auto-expires.
20	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You give me a label and a value and say 'keep this for 60 seconds,' and I store it and forget it on time automatically."
21	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the write has to land on the key's owning node *and* be replicated so a node crash doesn't lose it — and we have to decide whether the client waits for replicas (slow, safe) or not (fast, lossy).
22	
23	**FR3 — EXPIRE / TTL and eviction.** Keys must expire on their TTL, and when a node fills up its RAM, it must evict (LRU/LFU) to make room rather than crash.
24	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Old or unused stuff gets thrown out automatically so we never run out of room — like a fridge that quietly tosses the oldest leftovers when it's full."
25	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** tracking 'least recently used' precisely across millions of keys is itself memory- and CPU-expensive, so we approximate; and expiry of a *popular* key is what triggers the stampede deep dive.
26	
27	**FR4 — Partitioning across nodes (consistent hashing).** Keys must be spread across many nodes, and adding or removing a node must move only a small slice of keys, not reshuffle everything.
28	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When we add a new machine to the pool, only a tiny fraction of the data has to move — the rest stays put — so the cache doesn't go cold."
29	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** naive `hash(key) % N` remaps ~all keys when N changes — a cold-cache catastrophe that hammers the origin DB. Consistent hashing is the whole point.
30	
31	**FR5 — Replication + failover.** Each shard's data must have copies on other nodes, and if the primary for a shard dies, a replica must be promoted automatically with seconds of downtime, not minutes.
32	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Every piece of data lives on more than one machine, so if one dies, another instantly takes over and you barely notice."
33	- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** promoting a replica requires agreeing *who's in charge now* without two nodes both thinking they're primary (split-brain) — that needs a coordinator and a quorum.
34	
35	### Below the line (out of scope)
36	
37	- Multi-key transactions / Lua scripting atomicity across shards (single-key only here).
38	- Durable persistence as a system-of-record (we're a cache; the DB behind us is the source of truth — we'll keep an AOF/snapshot for warm restart only).
39	- Secondary indexes / range queries / full-text search (this is a KV store, not a database).
40	- Cross-region active-active replication (we'll mention it; primary design is single-region multi-AZ).
41	- Pub/sub, streams, geo commands (Redis has them; not core to "a cache").
42	
43	### Non-Functional requirements
44	
45	**NFR1 — Latency: p99 < 1 ms on a cache HIT (intra-AZ), p99 < 5 ms including a client→router→node round trip.**
46	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 out of 100 lookups come back faster than you can blink — under a millisecond at the node."
47	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if the cache is slow, callers fall back to the database, the whole point of the cache evaporates, and the DB — which we sized assuming the cache absorbs 90% of reads — gets crushed.
48	
49	**NFR2 — Availability: 99.99% on the read path ("four nines" ≈ 52 minutes of downtime per year).**
50	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The cache is essentially always up — under an hour of total outage across a whole year."
51	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** every minute the cache is unavailable, the origin DB eats the full unshielded read load (10x–100x its provisioned capacity) and likely falls over too — a correlated outage.
52	
53	**NFR3 — Scalability: linear horizontal scale to ~10M ops/s, elastic — add nodes without a maintenance window.**
54	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Need more throughput? Add machines and it just goes faster, no downtime."
55	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** traffic growth forces a forklift migration; or worse, a single node becomes the ceiling and we can't grow past it.
56	
57	**NFR4 — Consistency: read-your-own-writes from the perspective of a single client path; bounded staleness (~single-digit ms) on replica reads.**
58	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you write something then immediately read it back, you see your own write; other readers might see the old value for a few milliseconds."
59	- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a user updates their profile, refreshes, and sees the stale version — looks like a bug; worse for things like rate-limit counters that depend on read-your-own-writes.
60	
61	**NFR5 — Durability stance: this is a cache, not a system of record — losing recent writes on a hard crash is acceptable (RPO seconds), because the DB behind us can refill.**
62	- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If a machine dies and we lose its last second of writes, that's OK — the real data lives in the database; the cache just refills."
63	- 💥 **What breaks without it (if we over-engineer durability):** forcing fsync-on-every-write would tank our latency to disk-speed and defeat the purpose of an in-memory cache.
64	
65	> ⚠️ **Gap I'd flag out loud:** "True read-your-own-writes across an async replica is not free — if a client writes to the primary then a load-balanced read lands on a lagging replica, it can read stale. I'll handle it with read-from-primary-after-write or a version token, and I'll call out the bounded staleness window in §7."
66	
67	> 💬 "The headline tension is this: a cache is only valuable if it's faster than the thing behind it AND absorbs almost all the load. So every decision — partitioning, replication, eviction — has to preserve sub-millisecond reads while keeping the fleet available and the origin shielded."
68	
69	> 🎙️ **Script:** "So at the core this is a sharded in-memory key-value store. The functional surface is tiny — get, put with a TTL, and expiry — but the system work is huge: I need to partition keys across hundreds of nodes with consistent hashing so adding a node moves almost nothing, replicate each shard so a death is invisible, evict gracefully when RAM fills, and survive the two killers of any cache — a single hot key and a stampede when a popular key expires. My non-functionals are sub-millisecond reads, four-nines availability, linear scale to ten million ops a second, and bounded staleness. Durability I deliberately keep loose — this is a cache, the database is the source of truth."
70	
71	---
72	
73	## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions
74	
75	**Q1 — "Is this a side cache the application talks to explicitly (cache-aside), or an inline/transparent cache that proxies the database?"**
76	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It completely changes who owns cache-miss logic — if it's cache-aside, the *app* reads the DB on a miss and repopulates; if it's inline, *we* do, which means we own the stampede problem entirely."
77	- ↔️ **Design fork:**
78	  - If cache-aside → client library handles miss→DB→set; we're a pure KV store; stampede mitigation is client-side (single-flight in the client lib).
79	  - If read-through/write-through → we proxy the DB, own miss-fill and write-back; heavier but client is dumb.
80	- **Assumption:** Cache-aside is the dominant real-world pattern (it's how Redis/Memcached are used), so I'll design a pure KV store and put stampede defense in a smart client library — but I'll design the server hooks so read-through is addable.
81	
82	**Q2 — "What's the read:write ratio and the peak aggregate throughput we're targeting?"**
83	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Caches are read-heavy; if it's 90/10 I optimize replicas-for-reads and treat writes as the cheaper path. The absolute number decides shard count."
84	- ↔️ **Design fork:**
85	  - If read-heavy (90/10) → scale by adding read replicas per shard; primary handles writes only.
86	  - If write-heavy → replicas don't help reads; I'd shard more aggressively and consider write-coalescing.
87	- **Assumption:** ~10M ops/s peak, 90% reads / 10% writes. This drives ~128 shards and replica-for-reads.
88	
89	**Q3 — "How skewed is the key distribution — is there a celebrity/hot-key problem?"**
90	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Uniform keys are easy — just shard. But if one key takes 5% of all reads, that single key alone outpaces a whole node, and no amount of sharding helps because one key lives on one shard."
91	- ↔️ **Design fork:**
92	  - If uniform → consistent hashing alone is enough.
93	  - If skewed (Zipfian) → I need hot-key mitigation: replicate the hot shard, client-side caching, and detection — this becomes a headline deep dive.
94	- **Assumption:** Heavily skewed (Zipfian) traffic — realistic for any cache fronting popular content. The hot key is my binding bottleneck.
95	
96	**Q4 — "How strong does consistency need to be — is stale data for a few milliseconds acceptable, or do we need strong consistency?"**
97	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It decides synchronous vs asynchronous replication, which is a 10x latency difference. A cache usually tolerates staleness; a distributed lock built on the cache does not."
98	- ↔️ **Design fork:**
99	  - If eventual is fine → async replication, read from replicas, fast and cheap.
100	  - If strong needed → synchronous replication / quorum reads, much slower.
101	- **Assumption:** Eventual consistency with bounded staleness (~5 ms) and read-your-own-writes via read-from-primary. It's a cache.
102	
103	**Q5 — "Do we need automatic failover and elastic resharding without downtime, or is a maintenance window acceptable?"**
104	- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Automatic failover needs a coordinator and a quorum to avoid split-brain — real engineering. If a 5-minute window is OK, I can do manual promotion and save complexity."
105	- ↔️ **Design fork:**
106	  - If no-downtime required → cluster manager (Sentinel/Raft-based) for auto-failover + online slot migration.
107	  - If windows OK → manual ops, much simpler.
108	- **Assumption:** Automatic failover required (seconds RTO) and online resharding — this is a shared infra tier; downtime hits everyone.
109	
110	> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my big five. I'm going to proceed with cache-aside, ~10M ops/s at 90/10 reads, heavily skewed traffic, eventual consistency with read-your-own-writes, and automatic failover. Does that match what you had in mind, or do you want to push on any of those before I size it?"
111	
112	---
113	
114	## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)
115	
116	I computed these by writing and running `capacity.py`. Below are the rounded results. (Glosses: **QPS / ops/s** — operations per second hitting the fleet; **shard** — one slice of the keyspace owned by one primary node; **RF** — replication factor, how many copies of each shard exist; **TTL** — time-to-live, how long a key lives before auto-expiry.)
117	
118	| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
119	|---|---|---|---|---|
120	| Peak aggregate ops/s | given target | 10M ops/s | "about ten million ops a second" | overall fleet size |
121	| Peak reads/s | 10M x 0.90 | 9M reads/s | "nine million reads a second" | replica count (reads scale on replicas) |
122	| Peak writes/s | 10M x 0.10 | 1M writes/s | "a million writes a second" | primary throughput per shard |
123	| Per-node ceiling | conservative single-thread small GET/SET | ~100K ops/s | "a node tops out around a hundred thousand ops" | the unit we divide by |
124	| Primary shards | 10M ÷ 100K = 100, +30% headroom ≈ 130 → round | 128 shards | "about a hundred and twenty-eight shards" | partitioning scheme size |
125	| Per-shard load | 10M ÷ 128 | ~78K ops/s | "roughly eighty thousand per shard" | confirms headroom under 100K ceiling |
126	| Total nodes | 128 shards x 3 copies (1 primary + 2 replicas) | 384 nodes | "just under four hundred nodes" | cost + ops footprint |
127	| Dataset (1 copy) | 5B keys x (50+500 B) x 1.5 overhead = 825 B/entry | ~4.1 TB | "about four terabytes of hot data" | RAM sizing |
128	| Total RAM (x3) | 4.1 TB x 3 copies | ~12.4 TB | "around twelve terabytes of RAM" | dominant cost driver |
129	| RAM per node | 12.4 TB ÷ 384 | ~32 GB | "32-gig boxes" | instance type |
130	| **Hot key QPS** | **9M reads x 5% on one key** | **~450K QPS** | **"the hot key alone is ~450K a second"** | **THE binding bottleneck** |
131	| Stampede multiple | 450K reqs hit a ~5K-QPS DB key class | ~90x | "ninety times the DB can take" | single-flight + early recompute |
132	| Rebalance (consistent hash) | +1 node moves ~1/N = 1/128 | ~0.8% keys move | "under one percent moves" | consistent hashing vs mod-N |
133	| Rebalance (naive mod N) | +1 node moves (N-1)/N | ~99% keys move | "nearly everything moves" | why NOT mod-N |
134	| Failover RTO | 5s detect + 1s promote | ~6 s/shard | "about six seconds to fail over" | coordinator design |
135	| Monthly RAM cost | 12,375 GB x ~$6/GB-mo all-in | ~$75K/mo | "around seventy-five grand a month, mostly RAM" | cost optimization target |
136	
137	**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **hot key at ~450K QPS** against a **~100K ops/s node ceiling** — that's a **~4–5x single-node overload that sharding cannot fix** (one key lives on one shard). **Flip threshold:** if the hottest key stays under ~80K QPS (i.e. no key exceeds ~1% of read traffic), a single replica suffices and I can skip hot-shard replication and client-side caching entirely. Above that, hot-key mitigation becomes mandatory — so it leads my deep dives.
138	
139	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is the hot key. We're at about nine million reads a second, and with skewed traffic one celebrity key can grab five percent of that — call it 450 thousand requests a second to a single key. A node tops out around a hundred thousand. So that one key overloads its node four or five times over, and I can't shard my way out because a key lives on exactly one shard. That's my binding bottleneck, and it's why hot-key mitigation is my first deep dive. Storage is a non-issue — about twelve terabytes of RAM across the fleet, roughly seventy-five grand a month, dominated by memory, not compute."
140	
141	---
142	
143	## 4. Core Entities
144	
145	> This is a first-draft entity list — I'll add full field definitions in §7.
146	
147	**Entry** — a single key-value pair. Owns the value bytes, its TTL/expiry timestamp, and access metadata (last-access time / frequency counter) used for eviction.
148	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Think of it like a sticky note on a board — a label, what's written on it, and a 'throw away after' date."
149	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you store the TTL without scanning everything to find expired keys?" → "Lazy expiry on access plus a background sampling expirer — we don't scan, we sample."
150	
151	**Shard (Partition / Slot range)** — a contiguous slice of the hash ring / a set of hash slots. Owns a subset of all keys and maps to exactly one primary node at a time.
152	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Like one drawer in a giant filing cabinet — every label belongs to exactly one drawer, decided by its hash."
153	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What decides which shard a key lands in?" → "The key's hash position on the consistent-hashing ring (we use hash slots, 16,384 of them, mapped to shards)."
154	
155	**Node (Cache server)** — a physical/VM process holding one or more shards in RAM. A node is either a **primary** (accepts writes for its shards) or a **replica** (read-only copy that can be promoted).
156	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "One machine in the warehouse — it physically holds several drawers and can serve copies of others."
157	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What happens when a node dies?" → "A replica of each shard it owned gets promoted to primary by the cluster coordinator within seconds."
158	
159	**Cluster Topology / Slot Map** — the authoritative mapping of {hash slot → primary node + replica set}, plus node health. Owned by the cluster coordinator; gossiped to all nodes and cached by clients.
160	- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "The directory map taped to the warehouse wall: which drawer is in which machine, who's the boss of each drawer right now."
161	- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Isn't the topology a single point of failure / central directory on every read?" → "No — clients cache the map and route directly; they only refetch on a MOVED redirect or topology-change gossip, so reads never hit a central service."
162	
163	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four entities. An Entry is the key-value-plus-TTL — the sticky note. A Shard is a slice of the keyspace, a drawer. A Node is a machine holding drawers, and it's either a primary that takes writes or a replica that can be promoted. The one people underestimate is the Cluster Topology — the slot map — because that's what lets clients route directly to the right node without a central lookup on every read; clients cache it and only refresh on a redirect. I'll flesh out fields in the data model."
164	
165	---
166	
167	## 5. API / Interface
168	
169	The wire protocol is a thin binary/RESP-style command set, but I'll express it as logical operations. The client library holds the topology and routes directly to the owning node.
170	
171	### GET — read a key (satisfies FR1)
172	
173	- **Method + path:** `GET /v1/keys/{key}` (logically `GET key`). GET because it's a pure read with no state change — safe and cacheable at the client.
174	- **Key fields:** `key: string` (the only required input). `consistency: enum {primary|replica}` *(optional, default replica)* — lets a caller force read-from-primary for read-your-own-writes. The owning node is **not** in the request — the client computes it from the cached slot map; never trust a client-supplied node id.
175	- **Example:**
176	```
177	// Request
178	GET /v1/keys/user:42:profile?consistency=replica
179	
180	// Response 200 (hit)
181	{ "key": "user:42:profile", "value": "{...}", "ttlSeconds": 47, "node": "shard-13-replica-2" }
182	
183	// Response 404 (miss)
184	{ "key": "user:42:profile", "found": false }
185	```
186	- **Key design decision:** a **miss is a normal 404, not an error** — the client then reads the DB and repopulates (cache-aside). The `consistency` knob is the read-your-own-writes lever.
187	
188	### PUT / SET — write a key with optional TTL (satisfies FR2, FR3)
189	
190	- **Method + path:** `PUT /v1/keys/{key}`. PUT because it fully replaces the value at that key — idempotent by definition (same PUT twice = same state).
191	- **Key fields:** `value: bytes` (the payload); `ttlSeconds: int` *(optional)* — server computes the absolute expiry = `now + ttl`; the client sends a *relative* TTL, the **server sets the absolute timestamp** so clock skew on the client can't poison expiry. `waitForReplicas: int` *(optional, default 0)* — how many replica acks to wait for (0 = fire-and-forget, fast; 1+ = safer, slower).
192	- **Example:**
193	```
194	// Request
195	PUT /v1/keys/user:42:profile
196	{ "value": "{...}", "ttlSeconds": 60, "waitForReplicas": 1 }
197	
198	// Response 200
199	{ "key": "user:42:profile", "version": 7, "replicasAcked": 1, "expiresAt": "2026-06-21T10:00:60Z" }
200	```
201	- **Key design decision:** `waitForReplicas` makes the durability/latency trade-off **per-write**, not global — a rate-limit counter can demand an ack; a page-cache entry can fire-and-forget. `version` is a server-set monotonic token the client can echo on a later read for read-your-own-writes; **client never sets version or expiresAt.**
202	
203	### DELETE / EXPIRE — remove or re-TTL a key (satisfies FR3)
204	
205	- **Method + path:** `DELETE /v1/keys/{key}` to remove; `PATCH /v1/keys/{key}` with `{ "ttlSeconds": N }` to change TTL. DELETE removes state; PATCH partially updates (just the TTL).
206	- **Key fields:** `key` only for DELETE. DELETE is idempotent — deleting a missing key returns 200, not an error.
207	- **Example:**
208	```
209	// Request
210	DELETE /v1/keys/user:42:profile
211	// Response 200
212	{ "key": "user:42:profile", "deleted": true }
213	```
214	- **Key design decision:** DELETE is broadcast to replicas asynchronously like any write; a redundant delete is a no-op, which keeps client retries safe.
215	
216	### Admin — topology & resharding (satisfies FR4, FR5)
217	
218	- **Method + path:** `POST /v1/cluster/nodes` (add a node → triggers slot migration); `GET /v1/cluster/topology` (clients fetch/refresh the slot map). POST creates a new cluster member.
219	- **Security note:** **the caller is identified from a service JWT / mTLS, never a client-supplied identity.** Cluster-admin endpoints require an infra role; data-plane GET/PUT require a tenant scope. We never trust a client-supplied node id, version, or expiry timestamp — those are server-authoritative.
220	
221	> 💬 "Notice the client never tells us which node to hit and never sets the version or expiry — it computes routing locally from the cached map, and the server stamps the authoritative metadata."
222	
223	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The data-plane API is tiny: GET a key, PUT a value with an optional TTL, DELETE or re-TTL. The two senior touches: first, the client routes itself — it caches the slot map and computes the owning node, so reads never hit a central directory. Second, every mutating call has a server-set version and a server-computed absolute expiry, and PUT lets you choose per-write how many replicas to wait for — so a rate-limit counter can demand a replica ack while a page cache fires and forgets. Admin endpoints for adding nodes and fetching topology are locked behind an infra role."
224

227	## 6. High-Level Design (one operation at a time)
228	
229	Shared color language across all diagrams: blue = clients, green = our cache services, amber = async/replication, grey = datastores.
230	
231	### 6.1 "GET a value by key"
232	
233	The simplest slice, but it establishes the routing model that everything else builds on: the client finds the owning node *without* a central lookup.
234	
235	**Architecture decisions table**
236	
237	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
238	|---|---|---|---|---|
239	| Smart client library | A thin library in the caller that caches the slot map and routes each key directly to its owning node | Eliminates a central router hop on every read — keeps p99 under 1 ms | Not a proxy tier in front (Twemproxy/Envoy): adds a network hop + a thing to scale and fail | Clients must hold and refresh the topology map |
240	| Cache node (primary/replica) | An in-memory hash map process holding shards in RAM | RAM-speed reads; that's the entire product | Not on-disk KV (RocksDB): disk is ~1000x slower, defeats the purpose | Data is volatile; RAM is expensive |
241	| Read replica | A read-only copy of a shard a node can serve GETs from | Scales reads horizontally per shard for hot data | Not primary-only reads: would cap read QPS at one node per shard | Replica can be a few ms stale |
242	| Slot map (topology) | The {slot → primary + replicas} directory, gossiped | Lets clients self-route; one source of truth for ownership | Not per-request lookup to a directory service: central read hotspot | Clients can be briefly stale → handled by MOVED redirect |
243	
244	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
245	> **Consistent hashing / hash slots** — instead of `hash(key) % N`, we map every key onto a fixed ring of 16,384 slots, and slots are assigned to nodes. Adding a node just reassigns *some slots*, not all keys. Here it's what makes the cache elastic.
246	> **Slot map** — the table of which node owns which slots. Here clients cache it so they route directly and reads stay sub-millisecond.
247	> **MOVED redirect** — if a client routes to a node that no longer owns a slot (stale map), the node replies "that slot moved to node X." Here it's the self-correcting mechanism for stale topology.
248	> **Read replica** — a passive copy of a shard that serves reads. Here it multiplies read throughput for hot shards.
249	
250	**Diagram — GET hot path**
251	```mermaid
252	flowchart TD
253	    Client["Client app + smart lib"]
254	    Client -->|"1 hash key to slot"| SlotMap["Slot map cached in client"]
255	    Client -->|"2 GET routed to owner"| Primary["Cache primary shard 13"]
256	    Client -.->|"2b GET from replica"| Replica["Cache replica shard 13"]
257	    Primary -->|"3 value or miss"| Client
258	    Replica -.->|"3b value or miss"| Client
259	    Client -.->|"4 on miss read origin"| DB[("Origin datastore")]
260	    Client -.->|"5 repopulate PUT"| Primary
261	    Primary -.->|"6 MOVED if stale map"| Client
262	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
263	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
264	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
265	    class Client client
266	    class Primary,Replica,SlotMap svc
267	    class DB store
268	```
269	↳ reuses existing: none — this is the foundation slice.
270	
271	**Numbered step narration**
272	1. **Hash key → slot.** The client library hashes the key into one of 16,384 slots. *Why client-side:* avoids a network hop to a router. *State change:* none — pure computation. *What if the map is stale:* the slot it computes may point at the wrong node → handled in step 6.
273	2. **Route GET to owning node.** The client opens a connection straight to the primary (or a replica for `consistency=replica`). *Why direct:* sub-millisecond p99 demands no extra hop. *State:* read, no change. *What if the node is down:* client fails over to a replica connection and refreshes topology.
274	3. **Node returns value or miss.** In-memory hash lookup. *Why:* RAM lookup is ~100 ns. *State:* updates the key's last-access time for LRU. *What if miss:* returns 404.
275	4. **On miss, client reads the origin DB** (cache-aside). *Why client:* cache-aside puts miss logic in the caller. *State:* DB read. *What if a popular key misses at once:* stampede — deep dive §8.4.
276	5. **Client repopulates** via PUT with a TTL. *State:* key now cached. *What if two clients repopulate:* PUT is idempotent, last-writer-wins by version.
277	6. **MOVED redirect** if the client's map was stale. *State:* client refreshes its slot map and retries. *Why:* self-healing routing without a central directory.
278	
279	> 💬 "The key insight: clients route themselves using a cached map, so reads are one hop to RAM — and if the map is wrong, the node tells the client where to go instead of failing."
280	
281	> 🎙️ **Script:** "On a read, the client hashes the key to one of sixteen thousand slots, looks up the owning node in its locally cached slot map, and hits that node directly — one hop, RAM lookup, sub-millisecond. No central router. If the client's map is stale because we just reshuffled, the node replies MOVED with the correct node and the client refreshes and retries. On a miss it's cache-aside: the client reads the database and writes the value back with a TTL. The trade-off is clients carry the topology, but that's exactly what buys us the no-extra-hop latency."
282	
283	### 6.2 "PUT a value with optional TTL"
284	
285	The write path adds replication: a write isn't done until it's safely on the primary and propagating to replicas.
286	
287	**Architecture decisions table**
288	
289	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
290	|---|---|---|---|---|
291	| Async replication stream | Primary streams each write to its replicas in the background | Keeps write latency at RAM speed (no waiting on replicas by default) | Not synchronous quorum on every write: ~10x latency, kills the cache value prop | Replicas lag a few ms → bounded staleness |
292	| Per-write wait-for-replicas | The `waitForReplicas` knob makes some writes wait for N acks | Lets correctness-critical writes opt into durability without slowing all writes | Not global sync: one-size-fits-all is wrong for a cache | Caller must know which writes matter |
293	| Expiry subsystem | Lazy-on-access + background sampling expirer for TTLs | Avoids scanning millions of keys to find expired ones | Not a precise per-key timer: millions of timers is memory + CPU death | A few expired keys linger briefly until sampled |
294	
295	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
296	> **Async replication** — the primary acks the write immediately and ships it to replicas in the background. Here it preserves sub-millisecond writes.
297	> **Replica lag** — the short window where a replica hasn't yet applied a write. Here it's our staleness bound (~5 ms).
298	> **Lazy + sampling expiry** — we delete an expired key when someone touches it (lazy), and a background job samples random keys to catch the rest. Here it keeps expiry cheap.
299	
300	**Diagram — PUT / write path**
301	```mermaid
302	flowchart TD
303	    Client["Client app + smart lib"]
304	    Client -->|"1 PUT routed to primary"| Primary["Cache primary shard 13"]
305	    Primary -->|"2 write to RAM map"| Primary
306	    Primary -.->|"3 stream write async"| Replica1["Replica A shard 13"]
307	    Primary -.->|"3 stream write async"| Replica2["Replica B shard 13"]
308	    Primary -->|"4 ack with version"| Client
309	    Replica1 -.->|"5 ack if waitForReplicas"| Primary
310	    Primary -.->|"6 append to AOF"| AOF[("Append-only file local")]
311	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
312	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
313	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
314	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
315	    class Client client
316	    class Primary svc
317	    class Replica1,Replica2 async
318	    class AOF store
319	```
320	↳ reuses existing: Client, Primary, Replica (from §6.1).
321	
322	**Numbered step narration**
323	1. **PUT routed to the primary.** Writes only go to the slot's primary. *Why:* a single writer per shard avoids write conflicts. *State:* none yet. *What if primary is down:* client gets a topology refresh; a promoted replica becomes the new primary.
324	2. **Write to the in-memory map** and bump version. *State:* value live and readable on the primary instantly (read-your-own-writes from primary). *What if RAM full:* eviction fires (§6.3).
325	3. **Stream the write asynchronously to replicas.** *Why async:* keeps write latency at RAM speed. *State:* replicas converge within ~5 ms. *What if a replica is slow:* it falls behind; backpressure / re-sync if it lags too far.
326	4. **Ack the client with the new version** (default, no replica wait). *State:* client holds a version token for read-your-own-writes.
327	5. **If `waitForReplicas≥1`, wait for replica acks** before step 4. *Why:* the per-write durability knob. *What if no replica acks in time:* return a partial-durability warning; caller decides.
328	6. **Append to local AOF** (append-only file) for warm restart only. *Why:* a rebooted node refills fast instead of cold. *State:* on-disk log. *Trade-off:* not a durability guarantee, just a warm-start optimization.
329	
330	> 💬 "Writes go to one primary per shard and replicate in the background — fast by default, with an opt-in to wait for replicas when the data actually matters."
331	
332	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A PUT always goes to the slot's single primary — one writer per shard means no write conflicts. The primary updates RAM, bumps a version, and acks immediately, streaming the change to its replicas in the background. By default we don't wait — that keeps writes at RAM speed — but a caller can set wait-for-replicas to one or more when the write is correctness-critical, like a counter. TTL is server-stamped as an absolute time so client clock skew can't poison it, and expiry is lazy plus background-sampled so we never scan the whole keyspace."
333	
334	### 6.3 "Expiry / eviction (TTL + LRU/LFU)"
335	
336	This slice keeps a node from ever overflowing RAM — the difference between graceful degradation and a crash.
337	
338	**Architecture decisions table**
339	
340	| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
341	|---|---|---|---|---|
342	| Approximate LRU/LFU eviction | When RAM hits a watermark, evict least-recently/frequently-used keys, sampled not exact | Exact LRU needs a global linked list touched on every access — too costly at 10M ops/s | Not exact LRU: per-access list maintenance halves throughput | Evicts a near-optimal, not perfect, victim |
343	| Maxmemory + policy | A RAM ceiling per node and a policy (allkeys-lru, volatile-ttl, noeviction) | Bounds memory so a node degrades, never OOM-crashes | Not unbounded: OOM kills the node and all its shards | Some keys evicted earlier than ideal |
344	
345	> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
346	> **LRU (Least Recently Used)** — evict the key untouched longest. Here it's the default victim-picking rule.
347	> **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** — evict the key accessed fewest times. Here it's better when access frequency matters more than recency.
348	> **Eviction watermark / maxmemory** — the RAM line at which eviction starts. Here it's the safety valve that prevents OOM.
349	
350	**Diagram — eviction**
351	```mermaid
352	flowchart TD
353	    Write["Incoming PUT"]
354	    Write -->|"1 check maxmemory"| Check{"RAM over watermark"}
355	    Check -->|"2 no store key"| Store["Store in RAM map"]
356	    Check -->|"3 yes sample N keys"| Sample["Sample candidate keys"]
357	    Sample -->|"4 pick LRU or LFU victim"| Evict["Evict victim key"]
358	    Evict -->|"5 free RAM then store"| Store
359	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
360	    class Write,Store,Sample,Evict svc
361	    class Check svc
362	```
363	↳ reuses existing: cache node internals (from §6.1/6.2).
364	
365	**Numbered step narration**
366	1. **On each write, check maxmemory.** *Why:* eviction is write-triggered, not a background sweep, so RAM is bounded exactly. *State:* none if under.
367	2. **Under watermark → just store.** Normal path.
368	3. **Over watermark → sample N random keys** (e.g. 5). *Why sampling:* approximating LRU/LFU over a sample avoids maintaining a global ordering on the hot path. *State:* none yet.
369	4. **Pick the LRU/LFU victim** from the sample. *State:* victim chosen.
370	5. **Evict and free RAM, then store the new key.** *State:* node stays under its ceiling. *What if a hot key gets evicted:* the sampling rarely picks hot keys (they're recently/frequently used), and client-side caching shields them anyway.
371	
372	> 💬 "Eviction is approximate-LRU on a small random sample — we never maintain a perfect ordering because at ten million ops a second that bookkeeping would itself be the bottleneck."
373	
374	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Eviction is what keeps a node from ever crashing on memory. We set a max-memory ceiling, and when a write would push past it, the node samples a handful of random keys and evicts the least-recently or least-frequently used among them. It's approximate on purpose — maintaining an exact LRU list would mean touching a global structure on every single access, which at our throughput would halve performance. The trade-off is we occasionally evict a slightly-sub-optimal key, which for a cache is completely fine."

415	## 7. Data Model & Storage
416	
417	### PART A — Entity table
418	
419	| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key) | Chosen store | Partition key | Consistency |
420	|---|---|---|---|---|
421	| Entry | **key (PK, shard key)**, value bytes, version (monotonic), expiresAt (abs ts), lastAccess, freqCounter | In-memory hash map on cache node (+ AOF) | `hash_slot(key)` | Strong on primary; eventual (~5 ms) on replicas |
422	| Shard | **slotRange (PK)**, primaryNodeId, replicaNodeIds, epoch | Coordinator metadata (Raft-backed) | slotRange | Strongly consistent (Raft) |
423	| Node | **nodeId (PK)**, host, role, healthState, lastHeartbeat | Coordinator metadata + gossip | nodeId | Strongly consistent |
424	| Slot map / Topology | **epoch (version)**, {slot → primary + replicas} | Coordinator (Raft) + gossiped + client-cached | epoch | Strong at coordinator; eventually consistent at clients (MOVED corrects) |
425	
426	### PART B — Storage decision cards
427	
428	**🗄️ In-memory hash map (the cache itself) — used for: Entry**
429	1. **What it is:** a process holding all its keys in RAM as a hash table — lookups in ~100 nanoseconds, no disk involved.
430	2. **Why — the access pattern that forces it:** the entire product promise is sub-millisecond reads absorbing 90% of traffic; only RAM is fast enough. A disk read is ~1000x slower and would make the cache pointless.
431	3. **Considered & rejected:**
432	   - **RocksDB / on-disk LSM KV** — durable and bigger-than-RAM, but reads hit SSD; ~100 µs–1 ms, an order of magnitude too slow for a hit.
433	   - **Memcached** — great simple cache, but no native replication/failover/cluster topology; we need those, so a Redis-style model wins.
434	4. **Trade-off:** data is volatile and RAM is the dominant cost (~$75K/mo); a hard crash loses recent un-replicated writes (acceptable per NFR5).
435	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "The cache is just an in-memory hash map per node — RAM-speed reads are the whole point. I keep a local append-only file purely so a rebooted node warms up fast, not as a durability guarantee — the database behind us is the source of truth."
436	
437	**🗄️ Coordinator metadata store (Raft-backed) — used for: Shard, Node, Slot map**
438	1. **What it is:** a small strongly-consistent store (a Raft consensus group — a handful of nodes that vote so they always agree on one value) holding the cluster's authoritative state.
439	2. **Why — the access pattern that forces it:** failover and resharding require *one* agreed answer to "who is primary for slot X right now" — two nodes both thinking they're primary (split-brain) corrupts data. Only consensus gives that.
440	3. **Considered & rejected:**
441	   - **Storing topology in the cache itself** — circular: the thing that fails is the thing tracking failures.
442	   - **A single ZooKeeper/etcd node** — single point of failure; we need a quorum of 3–5.
443	4. **Trade-off:** consensus writes are slow (~10s of ms), but topology changes are rare, so it doesn't touch the hot path.
444	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Cluster state lives in a small Raft group — three or five nodes that vote — so there's always exactly one agreed primary per shard. It's slow for writes, but topology changes are rare, and clients cache the result so the data path never touches it."
445	
446	**🗄️ Append-only file (AOF) — used for: Entry warm restart**
447	1. **What it is:** a local on-disk log of every write, replayed on reboot to refill RAM.
448	2. **Why:** lets a restarted node rejoin warm in seconds instead of cold-missing every key and stampeding the DB.
449	3. **Considered & rejected:** **no persistence** — a cold restart of a 32 GB node means 100% misses → origin overload; **fsync-per-write** — disk-speed latency, defeats the cache.
450	4. **Trade-off:** the AOF lags the in-memory state slightly (fsync every ~1 s), so a crash loses ~1 s of writes — fine for a cache.
451	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Each node keeps an append-only file flushed about once a second, just so a reboot comes back warm. It's not durability — it's anti-stampede insurance on restart."
452	
453	### PART C — Per-operation consistency
454	
455	| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
456	|---|---|---|---|
457	| PUT (default) | Primary RAM | Strong on primary, async to replicas | Read-your-own-writes from primary; replicas converge in ~5 ms |
458	| PUT (waitForReplicas≥1) | Primary + replicas | Stronger (acked by N replicas) | Counters / locks that can't tolerate loss on failover |
459	| GET (replica) | Replica RAM | Eventual, bounded ~5 ms | Display data tolerates few-ms staleness; multiplies read throughput |
460	| GET (primary, read-your-own-writes) | Primary RAM | Strong | A client that just wrote can force-read its own write |
461	| Topology change / failover | Coordinator (Raft) | Strongly consistent (quorum) | Exactly one primary per shard — no split-brain |
462	
463	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The cache itself is an in-memory hash map per node — RAM is the whole point, and a local append-only file just makes restarts warm. The one place I spend strong consistency is the control plane: cluster topology lives in a small Raft group so there's always exactly one agreed primary per shard and we never get split-brain. On the data path, reads default to replicas with about five milliseconds of staleness, but a client can force read-from-primary for read-your-own-writes, and a write can opt into waiting for replica acks. I considered putting topology in the cache itself and rejected it — that's circular, the thing tracking failures can't be the thing that fails."
464

467	## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great
468	
469	> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** "Go back to the binding bottleneck — one key at 450K QPS against a 100K-per-node ceiling — and reason forward: sharding can't help one key, so I either replicate that shard or cache it closer to the client."
470	
471	### How do we partition keys so adding/removing a node doesn't reshuffle everything?
472	
473	#### Bad: hash(key) modulo N
474	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Decide a key's node by taking its hash and dividing by the number of nodes, keeping the remainder."
475	**Approach:** `node = hash(key) % N`. Simple, uniform, one line of code.
476	**Why people try this:** it's the obvious first instinct and it *does* spread keys evenly.
477	**⚠️ What breaks:** the moment N changes, the divisor changes, so almost every key maps to a *different* node. Per my math, adding one node to 128 remaps **~99% of keys (~5 billion)** — every one becomes a miss, all of them stampede the origin DB simultaneously, and the cache effectively goes cold during a routine scale-up.
478	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any elastic cluster — we add/remove nodes routinely, and a 99% remap is a self-inflicted outage every time.
479	
480	#### Good: consistent hashing on a ring
481	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** mod-N ties every key's placement to the *total count* N, so changing N moves everyone. Consistent hashing ties a key only to its *neighbor on a ring*, so changing the count moves only neighbors.
482	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Lay all keys and all nodes on a big circle; a key belongs to the next node clockwise. Adding a node only steals keys from its immediate neighbor."
483	**Approach:** hash nodes and keys onto a 2^32 ring; a key is owned by the first node clockwise. Adding a node inserts it on the ring and it takes over only the arc between it and its predecessor — **~1/N of keys (~0.8%, ~39M)** move, not 99%.
484	**⚠️ What breaks:** with few nodes, placement is lumpy — one node can own a huge arc and get overloaded while another owns a sliver (load imbalance up to 2–3x).
485	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** uneven node load and the need for clean, fast rebalancing pushes us to virtual nodes / fixed hash slots.
486	
487	```mermaid
488	flowchart TD
489	    K["Key hashes to ring position"]

498	#### Great: virtual nodes + fixed hash slots
499	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** a plain ring gives each physical node one arc, so a few nodes create lumpy load and rebalancing is coarse. Splitting each node into many virtual points (or using fixed slots) makes load smooth and migration fine-grained.
500	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Give every machine hundreds of small spots around the circle instead of one big arc, so the load evens out and we can hand over data in tiny chunks."
501	**Approach:** use a fixed number of **hash slots** (16,384, the Redis Cluster model): `slot = CRC16(key) % 16384`, and assign slots to nodes. Each node owns ~128 slots. Adding a node = reassign some *slots* (and migrate just those keys) — fine-grained and online. Equivalently, virtual nodes: each physical node = ~200 ring points so arcs average out.
502	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 16,384 slots ÷ 128 nodes = 128 slots/node; adding one node moves ~128 slots' worth of keys = ~1/128 ≈ **0.8% of keys**, migrated slot-by-slot online while the rest keep serving. Compare mod-N's 99%.
503	**✅ Failure matrix:**
504	
505	| Scenario | What happens |
506	|---|---|
507	| Add node | ~128 slots migrate online; clients get MOVED, refresh map; no cold cache |
508	| Remove node | Its slots reassigned to peers; replicas already hold the data → near-instant |
509	| Client has stale map | Routes to old owner → MOVED redirect → refresh + retry |
510	| Migration in progress for a slot | Node replies ASK redirect for keys mid-move; client follows |
511	| Two nodes claim a slot | Coordinator epoch resolves — higher epoch wins, no split-brain |

524	> 🎙️ **Script:** "I'd never use hash-mod-N — adding one node remaps almost everything and the cache goes cold, hammering the database. Consistent hashing fixes the big problem: a key belongs to the next node clockwise on a ring, so adding a node only steals from its neighbor — about one percent moves. The production refinement is fixed hash slots — sixteen thousand of them — so each node owns a couple hundred slots, load is smooth, and I can migrate keys one slot at a time, online, while everything else keeps serving."
525	> 🧠 **If they ask "why 16,384 slots, not a million?":** "It's a tradeoff — slots are tracked in a bitmap gossiped between nodes, so more slots means a bigger heartbeat payload. Sixteen thousand is plenty granular for thousands of nodes while keeping the gossip message small."

527	### How do we survive a hot key (the binding bottleneck)?
528	
529	#### Bad: just shard harder
530	**🗣️ Plain words:** "If one key is too busy, add more nodes."
531	**Approach:** scale the cluster out.
532	**Why people try this:** sharding solves *aggregate* load, so it feels like it should solve this too.
533	**⚠️ What breaks:** a single key lives on a single shard. At ~450K QPS against a ~100K node ceiling, that one node is **4–5x overloaded** no matter how many *other* nodes exist. Sharding does nothing for a single hot key.
534	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** the key is physically on one node — we must either copy that data wider or stop the requests from reaching it.
535	
536	#### Good: replicate the hot shard
537	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** sharding spreads *different* keys; it can't spread *one* key. Replicating the hot shard makes multiple nodes able to serve the *same* key.
538	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Make extra read-only copies of just the busy slice and spread reads across them."
539	**Approach:** detect the hot shard (per-key QPS counters), spin up extra read replicas of that shard, and round-robin reads across them. 450K QPS ÷ 100K/node = **~5 replicas** serve the hot key.
540	**⚠️ What breaks:** replicas lag (stale reads), it costs 5x the RAM for that shard, and detection→provisioning takes seconds — a flash spike outpaces it. And writes to the hot key still funnel to one primary.
541

543	flowchart TD
544	    Clients["Many clients"] -->|"1 reads for hot key"| LB["Read load spread"]
545	    LB -->|"2 round robin"| R1["Hot replica 1"]
546	    LB -->|"2 round robin"| R2["Hot replica 2"]
547	    LB -->|"2 round robin"| R3["Hot replica 3"]
548	    P["Hot primary"] -.->|"3 async replicate"| R1
549	    P -.->|"3 async replicate"| R2
550	    P -.->|"3 async replicate"| R3
551	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
552	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
553	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3

555	    class LB,R1,P svc
556	    class R2,R3 async
557	```
558	
559	#### Great: client-side cache + hot-key detection
560	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** replicas still pull every read across the network to the cache tier and react too slowly for spikes. Caching the hot key *in the client's own memory* stops the requests at the source.
561	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Let each client keep its own tiny copy of the few hottest keys for a fraction of a second, so most repeat reads never leave the client at all."
562	**Approach:** the cache tracks per-key access frequency; when a key crosses a hotness threshold, it's flagged hot and clients are told to **locally cache it with a very short TTL** (e.g. 100 ms–1 s) plus **invalidation push** (the server notifies clients when the hot value changes — tracking/client-side caching, like RESP3 client-side caching). With a 95% client-cache hit rate, the residual to the tier is **450K x 5% ≈ 22.5K QPS** — a fraction of one node.
563	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** 450K QPS x (1 − 0.95) = 22.5K QPS residual = **0.2 of a node** — the hot key is now a non-event. Without it we'd need ~5 dedicated replicas just for one key.
564	**✅ Failure matrix:**
565	
566	| Scenario | What happens |
567	|---|---|
568	| Spike on a known-hot key | Served from client memory, 95% never reach tier |
569	| Hot value changes | Server pushes invalidation; clients drop their copy, next read refreshes |
570	| New flash-hot key (not yet detected) | Brief tier overload until detection (~1 s) → replicate hot shard as backstop |
571	| Client cache stale (missed invalidation) | Bounded by the short local TTL (≤1 s) → self-heals |
572

575	    Client["Client with local hot cache"]

577	    Client -.->|"2 miss go to tier"| Tier["Cache tier"]
578	    Tier -->|"3 flag key hot"| Detect["Hotness counter"]
579	    Tier -.->|"4 push invalidation on change"| Client
580	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3

585	
586	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The hot key is my binding bottleneck — one celebrity key can pull 450 thousand reads a second, and a node only does a hundred thousand, so sharding is useless because the key lives on one shard. First lever: replicate just that hot shard to about five read replicas. But the real fix is client-side caching with invalidation — the tier detects a key is hot, tells clients to keep their own copy for a fraction of a second, and pushes an invalidation when it changes. At a 95% client hit rate the tier only sees about 22 thousand a second — a fifth of one node. The hot key goes from a fire to a non-event."
587	> 🧠 **If they ask "what about a hot *write* key?":** "Reads are the usual hot-key shape and replicas/client-cache fix those. A hot write key still funnels to one primary — there I'd shard the logical key, like splitting a counter into N sub-counters summed on read, so the writes spread."

589	### How do we replicate and fail over without split-brain?
590	
591	#### Good: async primary-replica with manual failover
592	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Each shard has a boss copy and backup copies; if the boss dies, an operator promotes a backup."
593	**Approach:** primary streams writes to replicas async; on primary death, a human (or simple script) promotes a replica.
594	**⚠️ What breaks:** manual promotion means minutes of downtime, and a naive script can promote two replicas at once → **split-brain**: two primaries accept conflicting writes, and reconciliation loses data.
595	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** four-nines availability (52 min/yr) can't survive minutes of manual failover, and split-brain corrupts data.
596	
597	```mermaid
598	flowchart TD
599	    P["Primary shard"] -.->|"1 async stream writes"| R1["Replica 1"]
600	    P -.->|"1 async stream writes"| R2["Replica 2"]
601	    Op["Operator"] -->|"2 manual promote on death"| R1
602	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
603	    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
604	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
605	    class P svc
606	    class R1,R2 async
607	    class Op client
608	```
609	
610	#### Great: coordinator quorum + epoch fencing (auto-failover)
611	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** manual promotion is slow and split-brain-prone. A quorum-based coordinator detects failure fast and guarantees exactly one promotion via a monotonic epoch.
612	**🗣️ Plain words:** "A small committee of monitors votes that the boss is really dead before promoting exactly one backup, and stamps a new term number so the old boss can't sneak back in."
613	**Approach:** a coordinator (Raft group) heartbeats every node. A primary is declared down only when a **quorum** agrees (avoids a single flaky monitor causing needless failover). The coordinator promotes the most up-to-date replica and bumps the shard's **epoch** (term number). Any write/redirect carries the epoch; nodes reject anything with a stale epoch — so even if the old primary revives, it's **fenced out** (its epoch is lower). RTO ≈ 5 s detect + 1 s promote = **~6 s/shard**.
614	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** down-after-ms 5,000 + promote 1,000 = ~6 s per shard; with shards failing independently and clients retrying replicas during the window, user-visible impact is sub-second per affected key. 99.99% = 52 min/yr budget; a 6 s failover a few times a month spends only a tiny slice of it.
615	**✅ Failure matrix:**
616	
617	| Scenario | What happens |
618	|---|---|
619	| Primary crashes | Quorum detects in ~5 s → promote freshest replica → bump epoch → ~6 s RTO |
620	| Network blip (false positive) | Quorum requirement prevents promotion on one flaky monitor |
621	| Old primary revives | Fenced by epoch — its writes rejected, it resyncs as a replica |
622	| Replica behind at promotion | Promote the most-advanced replica; recent un-acked writes may be lost (RPO ~secs, acceptable for cache) |
623	| Coordinator partition | Raft minority can't promote → preserves safety over liveness |
624	
625	```mermaid
626	flowchart TD
627	    Coord["Coordinator Raft quorum"] -->|"1 heartbeat all nodes"| P["Primary shard"]
628	    Coord -->|"2 quorum says down"| Decide{"Promote"}
629	    Decide -->|"3 pick freshest replica"| R1["Replica 1 promoted"]
630	    Decide -->|"4 bump epoch fence old"| Epoch["New epoch number"]
631	    P -.->|"5 revived primary rejected"| Epoch
632	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
633	    class Coord,P,R1,Epoch svc
634	    class Decide svc
635	```
636	
637	> 🎙️ **Script:** "Failover is where you avoid split-brain. A small Raft coordinator heartbeats every node, but it only declares a primary dead when a quorum agrees — that stops a single flaky monitor from causing a needless failover. It promotes the most up-to-date replica and bumps an epoch, a term number; every message carries the epoch and nodes reject stale ones, so even if the old primary comes back it's fenced out and rejoins as a replica. End to end that's about six seconds per shard, and shards fail independently, so the user-visible hit is sub-second per key. We accept losing a second or two of un-replicated writes — it's a cache."
638	> 🧠 **If they ask "why not synchronous replication for zero data loss?":** "Sync replication on every write adds a cross-node round trip — roughly 10x the latency — and defeats the cache's reason to exist. For the rare write that can't lose, I expose wait-for-replicas per call instead of taxing all writes."

640	### How do we prevent a cache stampede (thundering herd)?
641	
642	#### Bad: plain TTL expiry
643	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Set a 60-second timer; when it pops, the key's just gone."
644	**Approach:** key expires at its TTL; the next reader misses and refills from the DB.
645	**Why people try this:** it's the textbook cache-aside pattern and works fine for cold keys.
646	**⚠️ What breaks:** for a *hot* key, the instant it expires, **every** concurrent reader misses at once. Per my math, ~450K requests hit a DB key class that handles ~5K QPS — a **~90x stampede** that takes down the origin, which then can't refill the cache, which causes more misses — a death spiral.
647	**🔁 What forces the upgrade:** any popular key with a TTL is a scheduled outage.
648	
649	#### Good: single-flight (request coalescing) + locking
650	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** plain expiry lets every misser independently hit the DB. Single-flight lets only *one* refill the value while the rest wait for it.
651	**🗣️ Plain words:** "When a popular key expires, only the first person is allowed to go fetch it; everyone else waits and gets that one fresh copy."
652	**Approach:** on a miss for a hot key, the first request acquires a short per-key lock (e.g. SETNX with a 5 s TTL), fetches from the DB, and repopulates; concurrent readers see the lock and either wait briefly or serve the slightly-stale old value. The DB sees **1 request instead of 450K**.
653	**⚠️ What breaks:** the lock holder can crash mid-fetch (lock TTL releases it, but a thundering retry can re-form); and *every* reader still momentarily stalls at the expiry instant.
654	
655	```mermaid
656	flowchart TD
657	    Many["450K concurrent readers"] -->|"1 key expired miss"| Lock{"Acquire per key lock"}
658	    Lock -->|"2 winner fetches DB"| DB[("Origin DB single read")]
659	    DB -->|"3 repopulate"| Cache["Cache"]
660	    Lock -->|"4 losers wait or serve stale"| Cache
661	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
662	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
663	    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
664	    class Many client
665	    class Lock,Cache svc
666	    class DB store
667	```
668	
669	#### Great: probabilistic early recompute + stale-while-revalidate
670	**↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** single-flight still stalls everyone at the expiry instant and depends on a lock. Early recompute makes the value get refreshed *before* it expires, so it never hard-expires under load.
671	**🗣️ Plain words:** "Refresh the popular value a little early, in the background, before it ever runs out — and if it does briefly expire, keep serving the old copy while one request quietly refreshes it."
672	**Approach:** two combined techniques. (1) **Probabilistic early expiration (XFetch):** as a key nears its TTL, each read has a small, growing probability of triggering an *early* background refresh — so one lucky reader refreshes ahead of the deadline and the key never hard-misses for the herd. (2) **Stale-while-revalidate:** if it does expire, serve the stale value immediately while a single background single-flight refreshes it — readers never block, the DB sees one refill. Combined with the client-side caching from the hot-key dive, the herd never forms.
673	**🔢 Decision-forcing math:** without it, expiry → 450K reqs ÷ 5K DB ceiling = 90x overload. With single-flight + early recompute, the DB sees ~1 refill per TTL window per key = effectively **0x stampede**; readers serve stale for at most the refill latency (~ms).
674	**✅ Failure matrix:**
675	
676	| Scenario | What happens |
677	|---|---|
678	| Hot key nears TTL | One reader probabilistically refreshes early; no hard expiry |
679	| Hot key hard-expires anyway | Stale-while-revalidate serves old value, one bg refill |
680	| Refill request fails | Keep serving stale past TTL (bounded grace), retry refill |
681	| Refill holder crashes | Lock TTL releases; next reader retries; no herd because others serve stale |
682	| Value genuinely missing in DB | Cache a short negative result to stop repeated DB hits |
683	
684	```mermaid
685	flowchart TD
686	    Read["Read hot key"] -->|"1 check time to expiry"| Prob{"Early refresh probability"}
687	    Prob -->|"2 maybe refresh early bg"| Refill["Single background refill"]
688	    Read -->|"3 if expired serve stale"| Stale["Stale value served"]
689	    Stale -.->|"4 one bg revalidate"| Refill
690	    Refill -->|"5 repopulate"| Cache["Cache fresh value"]
691	    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
692	    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
693	    class Read client
694	    class Prob,Refill,Stale,Cache svc
695	```
696	
697	> 🎙️ **Script:** "A stampede is what kills caches: a hot key with a TTL expires, and suddenly 450 thousand readers all miss and pile onto the database, which can take five thousand — a ninety-x overload and a death spiral. The first fix is single-flight: only one request is allowed to refill, the rest wait or serve stale, so the DB sees one read not 450 thousand. The better fix is probabilistic early recompute plus stale-while-revalidate — the value gets refreshed in the background a little before it expires, so it never hard-misses under load, and if it ever does, we serve the old copy while exactly one request quietly refreshes. The herd never forms."
698	> 🧠 **If they ask "what if the DB value really is gone?":** "Cache a short-lived negative result — a 'not found' marker with a few-second TTL — so a missing key can't itself become a stampede of repeated DB lookups."

701	
702	## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost
703	
704	### 9A — Availability targets
705	
706	- **Read path: 99.99% ("four nines" = ~52 minutes/year of downtime).** Mechanisms: every shard has 2 replicas across **multi-AZ** (multiple data centres in one region, so one building losing power doesn't take the shard down); clients fail reads over to replicas instantly; client-side caching of hot keys means even a tier blip is partly absorbed at the client.
707	- **Write path: 99.95% (~4.4 hours/year).** Writes need the live primary; during the ~6 s failover window for an affected shard, writes to *that shard's* keys briefly fail and retry. Only the keys on the failing shard are affected (1/128 of keyspace), and failover is automatic, so aggregate write availability stays high.
708	- **Control plane (coordinator): 99.99% via Raft quorum** — survives losing a minority of its nodes.
709	
710	### 9B — Per-dependency failure table
711	
712	| Component | What breaks if down | Graceful degradation — what the USER sees | Recovery |
713	|---|---|---|---|
714	| One cache primary | Writes + primary-reads for its ~128 slots stall | Reads auto-route to a replica (a few ms staler); writes to those keys retry for ~6 s | Coordinator promotes a replica (~6 s RTO) |
715	| One replica | Loses some read capacity for its shard | Slightly higher load on remaining replicas/primary; no user-visible error | Re-provision replica; resyncs from primary |
716	| Coordinator quorum loses minority | No effect on data path | Nothing — clients use cached topology, data plane untouched | Lost node rejoins, catches up via Raft log |
717	| Coordinator loses majority | Can't promote on new failures | Existing primaries keep serving; failover paused | Restore quorum; failovers resume |
718	| Origin DB down | Misses can't refill | Cache serves whatever it has; stale-while-revalidate extends grace; new keys 404 | DB recovers; refills resume |
719	| Whole AZ outage | ~1/3 of nodes gone | Surviving AZs hold replicas → promote; capacity dips, auto-scale adds nodes | Failed AZ returns; rebalance slots |
720	| Network partition (client↔tier) | Client can't reach cache | Client falls back to reading origin DB directly (cache-aside degrades to no-cache) | Partition heals; cache repopulates |
721	
722	### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class
723	
724	- **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much recent data we can lose on a crash. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until service is restored.
725	
726	| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
727	|---|---|---|---|
728	| Cached entries (default writes) | ~1–2 s (un-replicated/un-fsynced writes) | ~6 s/shard | Async replication + AOF warm restart; acceptable — DB is source of truth |
729	| Critical entries (waitForReplicas≥1) | ~0 (acked by a replica before return) | ~6 s/shard | Synchronous replica ack on that write |
730	| Cluster topology | 0 (Raft is durable + quorum-committed) | seconds | Raft log replication; quorum survives minority loss |
731	
732	> 🗣️ "Because we're a cache, I deliberately accept losing a second or two of writes on a hard crash — the database behind us refills. The one thing I protect with zero data loss is the topology, via Raft, because a wrong topology corrupts everything."
733	
734	### 9D — Cost
735	
736	Top 3 drivers:
737	1. **RAM (~$75K/mo, the dominant line)** — 12.4 TB of memory across 384 nodes (3 copies of a 4.1 TB dataset). Memory-optimized instances are charged largely by provisioned RAM; replication triples it. *Biggest optimization:* drop RF from 3 to 2 for non-critical shards, and **compress values** / use smaller TTLs to shrink the hot set — RF and dataset size are the two levers.
738	2. **Cross-AZ network transfer** — async replication ships every write across AZs; at 1M writes/s x 3 copies, inter-AZ egress adds up. *Optimization:* keep one replica in-AZ where possible; compress the replication stream.
739	3. **Compute/ops overhead** — 384 nodes to patch, monitor, and fail over; on-call and tooling. *Optimization:* fewer, larger nodes reduce node count (but raise blast radius per node — a trade-off).
740	
741	The single biggest cost lever is **replication factor** — going RF2 instead of RF3 cuts RAM cost ~33% but loses one layer of failover safety.
742	
743	> 🎙️ **Script:** "When a primary dies, the user barely notices — reads instantly route to a replica that's a few milliseconds staler, and writes to that one shard's keys retry for about six seconds while we promote. If the origin database goes down, the cache keeps serving what it has and extends stale values rather than going dark. The worst case is losing a whole availability zone — we lose a third of our nodes, but replicas in the surviving zones get promoted and we auto-scale back up. Cost-wise, it's almost all RAM — about seventy-five grand a month — and the biggest lever is replication factor."
744
