746	
747	## 10. Trade-off Ledger
748	
749	**🔀 Decision: Async replication vs synchronous quorum (by default)**
750	1. **Chose & why:** async replication by default — keeps writes at RAM speed, the cache's reason to exist.
751	2. **Gave up:** zero data loss on failover; replicas lag ~5 ms so a crash can lose un-acked writes.
752	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We pick a fast highway that occasionally drops a parcel over a slower one that guarantees delivery — and we add a 'registered mail' option for the rare parcel that matters."
753	4. **When this reverses:** if the cache stores correctness-critical state (distributed locks, idempotency keys, financial counters) as the *primary* use case, default to synchronous — or push everyone to `waitForReplicas≥1`.
754	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Async by default because it's a cache and speed is the point, but I expose per-write replica acks so the rare write that can't lose data gets it."
755	
756	**🔀 Decision: Smart client routing vs a proxy tier**
757	1. **Chose & why:** smart clients cache the slot map and route directly — eliminates a hop, keeps p99 sub-ms.
758	2. **Gave up:** thin clients; now every client language needs a maintained library that handles topology and MOVED/ASK redirects.
759	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We give each caller a smart map so it goes straight to the right shelf, instead of asking a clerk every time — but now we maintain that map logic in every language."
760	4. **When this reverses:** in a polyglot org where maintaining N client libraries is too costly, put a thin proxy (Envoy) in front and accept the extra hop.
761	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Clients route themselves for latency; the cost is a smart client library per language, which I'd swap for a proxy if we had too many languages to support."
762	
763	**🔀 Decision: Approximate LRU vs exact LRU eviction**
764	1. **Chose & why:** approximate (sampled) LRU/LFU — exact ordering would mean touching a global structure on every access at 10M ops/s.
765	2. **Gave up:** perfect victim selection; occasionally evicts a not-quite-coldest key.
766	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We pick a pretty-old item to throw out instead of provably the oldest, because checking 'provably oldest' on every access would slow everything down."
767	4. **When this reverses:** if hit-ratio is so margin-critical that a few percent matters more than throughput — rare; for a cache, throughput wins.
768	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "I use approximate LRU on a small sample because exact LRU bookkeeping would itself become the bottleneck."
769	
770	**🔀 Decision: RF3 multi-AZ vs RF2 (cost vs safety)**
771	1. **Chose & why:** RF3 across AZs — survives a full-AZ loss with a replica still standing.
772	2. **Gave up:** ~33% more RAM cost (~$25K/mo of the $75K).
773	3. **🗣️ Plain words:** "We keep three copies in three buildings so losing a whole building still leaves a spare, at the cost of a third more memory."
774	4. **When this reverses:** if the cache is purely volatile display data where a brief AZ-loss capacity dip is fine, drop to RF2 and save the RAM.
775	5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Three copies across zones for AZ-failure survival; if budget got tight on non-critical shards I'd drop to two."
776	
777	> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'm least dogmatic about: async replication — fast but lossy, reversible per-write with a replica-ack knob; smart clients — great latency but a library-per-language tax I'd trade for a proxy in a polyglot shop; approximate LRU — because exact would be the bottleneck; and RF3 — safety I'd trim to RF2 if cost bit and the data was purely volatile. Every one of these traces back to the same principle: it's a cache, so I spend on speed and availability and stay loose on durability — until a specific write says otherwise."
778	
779	---
780	
781	## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers
782	
783	**(1) Core / hardest part**
784	
785	**❓ "One key is getting 450 thousand requests a second — how do you serve it?"**
786	The mechanism: sharding can't help because one key lives on one shard, so I replicate that hot shard to ~5 read replicas AND push client-side caching — the tier flags the key hot and clients keep a local copy with a sub-second TTL plus server invalidation, cutting tier load ~95%.
787	🗣️ **In plain words:** "I make extra copies of just the busy slice, and I let each caller keep its own tiny copy for a fraction of a second so most reads never even leave them."
788	💬 **One-liner:** "Sharding can't split one key, so I copy the shard and cache it inside the clients — 450K becomes ~22K at the tier."
789	
790	**❓ "Walk me through how adding a node doesn't cause an outage."**
791	With consistent hashing via 16,384 fixed slots, adding a node reassigns ~128 slots (~0.8% of keys), migrated online slot-by-slot; clients hit MOVED/ASK redirects and refresh their map while everything else keeps serving.
792	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Only a tiny slice of data moves to the new machine, a chunk at a time, and the rest never notices."
793	💬 **One-liner:** "Hash slots mean a new node steals ~1% of keys online — not the 99% that mod-N would reshuffle."
794	
795	**(2) Failure**
796	
797	**❓ "What happens when a primary node dies?"**
798	A Raft coordinator detects it by quorum in ~5 s, promotes the freshest replica, and bumps an epoch so the dead node can't return as a second primary; RTO ~6 s, and reads route to replicas in the meantime.
799	🗣️ **In plain words:** "A committee confirms the boss is really down, promotes one backup, and stamps a new term number so the old boss can't sneak back."
800	💬 **One-liner:** "Quorum-detected, epoch-fenced promotion in ~6 seconds — no split-brain."
801	
802	**❓ "What if the origin database behind the cache goes down?"**
803	Misses can't refill, but the cache keeps serving everything it already holds and stale-while-revalidate extends grace on expiring keys; only genuinely-new keys return misses.
804	🗣️ **In plain words:** "The cache keeps handing out what it already has and holds onto things a bit longer instead of going dark."
805	💬 **One-liner:** "We degrade to 'serve what we have' — the cache shields the DB, so a DB blip is mostly invisible."
806	
807	**❓ "What if the coordinator loses quorum?"**
808	Existing primaries keep serving from their cached topology — the data plane never touches the coordinator — but new failovers pause until quorum is restored; we choose safety over liveness.
809	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Everything keeps running; we just can't promote new backups until the committee is healthy again."
810	💬 **One-liner:** "Data plane is independent of the coordinator, so a coordinator outage pauses failover, not traffic."
811	
812	**(3) Scale / hot-spot**
813	
814	**❓ "A flash sale makes one product's key explode — handle it."**
815	That's the hot-key path: detect the spike via per-key counters, replicate the hot shard, and push client-side caching with short TTL + invalidation; if the spike is faster than detection (~1 s), the replicas are the backstop.
816	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We spot the surge, copy that one item across more machines, and let clients cache it locally so the surge mostly hits their own memory."
817	💬 **One-liner:** "Detect, replicate the shard, cache in the client — the hot key stops being hot at the tier."
818	
819	**❓ "How do you scale reads vs writes?"**
820	Reads scale by adding replicas per shard (they serve reads); writes scale by adding shards (each shard's single primary handles its slice). At 90/10 we lean on replicas.
821	🗣️ **In plain words:** "More copies for reads, more drawers for writes."
822	💬 **One-liner:** "Replicas multiply reads, shards multiply writes — I size each independently."
823	
824	**(4) Consistency**
825	
826	**❓ "If I write then immediately read, will I see my write?"**
827	Yes if you read from the primary (default for read-your-own-writes) or echo your write's version token; a load-balanced replica read can be ~5 ms stale, so for that case we route the follow-up read to the primary.
828	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Read from the boss copy right after writing and you always see your own change; the backup copies catch up in a few milliseconds."
829	💬 **One-liner:** "Read-your-own-writes via read-from-primary or a version token; replicas are bounded ~5 ms stale."
830	
831	**❓ "Can two writers corrupt the same key?"**
832	No — each shard has exactly one primary (enforced by the epoch/quorum), so all writes to a key serialize through one node; last-writer-wins by version.
833	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Only one machine is allowed to write a given key at a time, so there's no tug-of-war."
834	💬 **One-liner:** "One primary per shard means single-writer serialization — no concurrent corruption."
835	
836	**(5) Security**
837	
838	**❓ "How do you stop a client from reading/writing keys it shouldn't, or forging identity?"**
839	Callers authenticate via mTLS / service JWT and carry a tenant scope checked on every data-plane op; the client never supplies its own identity, node id, version, or expiry — those are server-authoritative.
840	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Every caller proves who it is with a certificate, and the server — not the caller — decides what it's allowed to touch and stamps all the metadata."
841	💬 **One-liner:** "mTLS + tenant scoping; the server stamps version and expiry so clients can't forge state."
842	
843	**❓ "What stops one tenant from evicting another tenant's data with a flood?"**
844	Per-tenant key namespacing and per-tenant memory quotas/rate limits, so one noisy tenant's eviction pressure and QPS are bounded to their own slice.
845	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Each tenant gets its own labeled space and a memory and rate budget, so one can't crowd out another."
846	💬 **One-liner:** "Namespaced keys + per-tenant quotas isolate blast radius."
847	
848	**(6) Cost**
849	
850	**❓ "What's the most expensive part and how would you cut it?"**
851	RAM — ~$75K/mo for 12.4 TB across 384 nodes, tripled by RF3. The biggest lever is replication factor (RF3→RF2 saves ~33%) plus value compression and tighter TTLs to shrink the hot set.
852	🗣️ **In plain words:** "It's almost all memory, and three copies of everything; fewer copies or smaller values is where the savings are."
853	💬 **One-liner:** "RAM dominates; RF and dataset size are the two cost dials."
854	
855	**❓ "Isn't a 384-node fleet a lot of ops cost?"**
856	Yes — that's why I'd consider fewer, larger nodes to cut node count and on-call surface, accepting a larger blast radius per node as the trade-off.
857	🗣️ **In plain words:** "More machines means more to babysit; bigger machines mean fewer to babysit but more eggs per basket."
858	💬 **One-liner:** "Fewer bigger nodes cut ops cost but raise per-node blast radius — a deliberate trade."
859	
860	**(7) Extensibility**
861	
862	**❓ "How would you add cross-region replication later?"**
863	Add async region-to-region replication of the write stream with one write-region (single-master) to avoid cross-region write conflicts, and route reads to the local region; accept higher cross-region staleness.
864	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Ship writes to other regions in the background and let each region read locally, with one region as the writer to avoid conflicts."
865	💬 **One-liner:** "Single-write-region + async region streams + local reads — bolt-on, no data-plane change."
866	
867	**❓ "How would you support read-through / write-back instead of cache-aside?"**
868	The node already owns the slot and replication; add a pluggable origin connector so a miss triggers a server-side DB fetch and a write buffers and flushes to the DB — the topology and replication layers are untouched.
869	🗣️ **In plain words:** "We just teach the cache itself to talk to the database on a miss, instead of making the caller do it."
870	💬 **One-liner:** "Add an origin connector — the partitioning and replication layers don't change."
871	
872	**❓ "How would you add bigger-than-RAM datasets?"**
873	Add a tiered store: hot keys in RAM, cold keys spilled to local NVMe with the same hash-slot ownership, so capacity grows without growing RAM linearly.
874	🗣️ **In plain words:** "Keep the busy stuff in memory and let the rarely-touched stuff live on fast disk on the same node."
875	💬 **One-liner:** "RAM+NVMe tiering under the same slot map lets capacity outgrow memory."
876	
877	> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a sharded, in-memory key-value cache that fronts a slower datastore at sub-millisecond latency. The design splits into two halves. The read half is a fleet of in-memory nodes partitioned by consistent hashing into sixteen thousand slots, where smart clients cache the slot map and route themselves straight to the owning node, with replicas multiplying read throughput and client-side caching absorbing hot keys. The write-and-control half centers on the hardest problems: one primary per shard takes writes and streams them asynchronously to replicas, a Raft coordinator quorum-detects failures and promotes replicas with epoch fencing so there's never split-brain, and cache stampedes are killed by single-flight plus probabilistic early recompute so a popular key never melts the origin. The two halves connect through that coordinator, which gossips topology to nodes and clients and orchestrates failover and online resharding. Every hard decision traces back to one principle: it's a cache, so we spend our budget on speed and availability and stay deliberately loose on durability — until a specific write asks otherwise."
878
